advaita.rendering: Surrender all actions unto Me — Vāsudeva, the all-knowing inner Self — through the discriminative understanding (viveka-buddhi) that 'I act as a servant before the Lord.' Rid yourself of desire (āśā) and the sense of mine-ness (mamabhāva), knowing these bind the one who has not yet stabilized in jñāna. Fight, free of the fever (jvara) of grief — for this niṣkāma-karma is the preparatory ground from which direct knowledge of the Self arises.
advaita.bhashya_anchor: Śaṅkara glosses mayi as 'Vāsudeve parameśvare sarvajñe sarvātmani'; adhyātma-cetasā as 'viveka-buddhyā'; and vigata-jvaraḥ as 'vigata-santāpaḥ vigata-śokaḥ'. The servant-before-king simile ('bhṛtyavat') is his own.
advaita.doctrinal_distinctives: Sannyāsa here is mental relinquishment (nikṣepā), not renunciation of action itself; the verse explicitly commands continued action (yudhyasva). This prepares the mumukṣu for jñāna, not bhakti.

viśiṣṭādvaita.rendering: Place all actions in Me — the inner ruler (antaryāmin) who dwells within each ātman as its very body — recognizing by the śruti-established knowledge that I alone propel you as My own body-instrument. Desire-freed, ownership-freed, and fever-freed, perform all ordained duty as pure worship of the Supreme Person (paramapuruṣa), trusting that He who is worshipped through action will Himself liberate from bondage.
viśiṣṭādvaita.bhashya_anchor: Rāmānuja repeatedly cites the antaryāmin-brāhmaṇa (Bṛhadāraṇyaka 5.7) and Gītā 18.61 ('yantrārūḍhāni māyayā'). He glosses adhyātma-cetasā as awareness of the ātman's bodyhood to Bhagavān, not merely discriminative reason.
viśiṣṭādvaita.doctrinal_distinctives: Sannyāsa = recognizing that Paramapuruṣa is the true agent moving this ātman; all karma thereby becomes kainkarya (service). Distinct from Śaṅkara's jñāna-preparation framing — here devotion is the telos, not a stepping-stone.

dvaita.rendering: Surrender unto Me, Hari, all the actions that ignorance (bhrānti) has falsely attributed to the jīva — for Bhagavān alone is the true agent of all action. The adhyātma-cetasā is the mind oriented to 'Bhagavān acts; I am His instrument'; sannyāsa is that recognition; nirmama is the declaration 'I am not the doer.' Fight, knowing the doership never belonged to you.
dvaita.bhashya_anchor: Madhva defines adhyātma-cetasā as 'ātmānam adhikṛtya yac cetaḥ' — specifically Bhagavān as the true agent; sannyāsaḥ is explicitly glossed as 'bhagavān karoti iti'; nirmama as 'nāhaṃ karomi iti'.
dvaita.doctrinal_distinctives: Uniquely Dvaita: the jīva's doership is not merely to be surrendered but is metaphysically false from the start (bhrāntyā jīve adhyāropitāni). Sannyāsa is correction of a category error, not a volitional act of dedication.

śuddhādvaita.rendering: Through Sāṅkhya-understanding — seeing Me, the primary agent (mukhya sākṣāt-kartā), as the Supreme Deity (paradevatā) — surrender and offer (samarpya) all actions unto Me. The threefold relinquishment is precise: nirāśīḥ releases attachment to results; nirmamaḥ releases ownership; vigata-jvaraḥ releases the illusion of being the kartā. Without this triple surrender and offering (arpaṇa) to Me, no fruit accrues — siddhi arises from arpaṇa alone.
śuddhādvaita.bhashya_anchor: Vallabha cites his own nibandha: 'samarpanāt karmaṇāṃ ca siddhir bhavati nānyathā.' He reads sāṅkhya-cetasā (his gloss for adhyātma-cetasā) as Kṛṣṇa-centered awareness, not abstract discriminative knowledge.
śuddhādvaita.doctrinal_distinctives: Arpaṇa (offering) is the operative category, not mere mental sannyāsa. The threefold analysis (phalāsakti / mamāsakti / kartṛtva-abhimāna) is Vallabha's structural contribution — absent in Śaṅkara or Rāmānuja.

bhakti.rendering: You are not yet a knower of truth (tattva-vit), so act you must — but act thus: offer all actions to Me as the antaryāmin who governs you, holding the understanding 'I act in dependence on the inner ruler.' Be desire-free, treating all fruit as Mine alone and thus empty of mine-ness; free of the grief (śoka) that is here called jvara. Fight.
bhakti.bhashya_anchor: Śrīdhara glosses adhyātma-cetasā as 'antaryāmy-adhīno'haṃ karomi iti dṛṣṭyā'; vigata-jvaraḥ as 'tyakta-śokaḥ'. He frames the entire verse as instruction specifically calibrated to Arjuna who is not yet a jñānin.
bhakti.doctrinal_distinctives: Explicitly positions the verse as a concession to non-tattva-vit status — the bhakta acts with this orientation precisely because jñāna is not yet stable. Bridges the practical and the doctrinal without collapsing either.

advaita-bhakti.rendering: The mumukṣu who cannot yet renounce action should offer all worldly and Vedic karma to Bhagavān Vāsudeva — the omniscient inner ruler — through the understanding 'I am a servant before the Lord, acting under His governance.' Desire-free (niṣkāma), bereft of mine-ness toward body, sons, and kin, freed from the grief (śoka here named jvara) born of fearing disgrace or hell, fight. Note: nirmama and vigata-jvaraḥ apply to the battle specifically; bhagavad-arpaṇa and niṣkāmatva are universal to all karma for the mumukṣu.
advaita-bhakti.bhashya_anchor: Madhusūdana imports Śaṅkara's bhṛtya simile and Vāsudeva-glossing but then distinguishes: nirmama and tyakta-śoka apply to yuddha alone ('anyatra mamāśokayoḥ aprasak-tatvāt'); bhagavad-arpaṇa and niṣkāmatva are sādhāraṇa for all karma of a mumukṣu.
advaita-bhakti.doctrinal_distinctives: Methodologically Advaita but devotionally warm: jñāna is the telos, bhakti is the vehicle for the not-yet-liberated. The fine-grained scope analysis (which qualifiers apply only to battle vs. all karma) is Madhusūdana's own structural move.

so_what_questions: If Madhva holds that the jīva's doership was always false (adhyāropita), does 3.30's command to 'surrender' presuppose a volitional act that the metaphysics disallows — or is the command itself only Bhagavān speaking through His instrument?

Rāmānuja's antaryāmin reading makes the ātman a body of Bhagavān; does this dissolve the ethical weight of niṣkāma-karma, since the Supreme Person is the real worshipper and worshipped simultaneously?

Vallabha's triple relinquishment (phala / mamā / kartṛtva) is analytically sharper than any other bhāṣya here — does this structure appear elsewhere in the Gītā, and is it original to him or drawn from earlier Vaiṣṇava sources?

Śaṅkara treats this verse as karma-as-jñāna-preparation; Madhusūdana treats it as instruction for the mumukṣu who is not yet a jñānin — are these the same position or does Madhusūdana's devotional framing mark a genuine departure?

Vigata-jvaraḥ is glossed by all commentators as grief/sorrow, but Madhusūdana adds the specific source: 'fear of disgrace or hell' (durya śo-narak-apāta). Does this specificity change the verse's practical application for a householder versus a renunciant?

The verse is the first explicit 'surrender all action to Me' command — but mayi here is contested: Śaṅkara reads it as Vāsudeva-as-all-Self, Madhva as Hari-as-independent-agent, Rāmānuja as Paramapuruṣa-antaryāmin. Does this polysemy of mayi stabilize or destabilize the concept of karma-yoga across schools?

Śrīdhara's framing ('you are not yet tattva-vit, so act') implies the verse is a developmental prescription, not a final statement. Which schools would agree that 3.30 is superseded at a higher stage, and which treat it as permanently normative?

everyday_applications.advaita: Before beginning work each morning, pause and internally place the entire day's tasks before the Self as witness — 'I act as instrument; the results belong to That.' When agitation (jvara) arises at a difficult outcome, trace it to the residual sense of 'I did this' and return to the viveka-buddhi that never owned the action. This is not passivity but the discipline of discriminative action.
everyday_applications.viśiṣṭādvaita: In any relationship or service role, practice recognizing the other person as a body of Bhagavān being moved by Him — including yourself. Perform your duty as kainkarya (service-worship): the task is the same, the interior stance shifts from personal entitlement to offering. When anxiety about outcomes appears, recall: 'He who governs will also protect those who worship through action.'
everyday_applications.dvaita: When you catch yourself saying 'I built this' or 'I failed at this,' recognize the Dvaita diagnosis: doership was never yours — it was Hari's working through you. Use this not as an excuse for inaction but as radical humility: you are an instrument, and instruments do not take credit. Daily examination: 'Where today did I claim agency that belongs only to Hari?'
everyday_applications.śuddhādvaita: At the close of each action — a conversation, a meal prepared, a task completed — explicitly offer it: 'This is Yours, Kṛṣṇa.' Vallabha's arpaṇa is not a ceremony but a moment of conscious re-attribution. The threefold check: Did I want a specific result (phala-āsakti)? Did I treat this as mine (mamā)? Did I see myself as the cause (kartṛtva)? Each 'yes' is a practice opportunity, not a failure.
everyday_applications.bhakti: For the practitioner who knows they are not yet fully established in truth, Śrīdhara's framing is honest and practical: do not wait for enlightenment before acting rightly. Act with the antaryāmin orientation — 'I am governed' — and let that orientation gradually erode the grief (śoka) that comes from treating outcomes as personal verdicts. The verse is permission to act, not a demand for perfection first.
everyday_applications.advaita-bhakti: Madhusūdana's scope distinction applies directly: while cooking or working, keep the universal practice — offer the action, hold no result, desire nothing. But when specifically in conflict or competition (the 'battle'), the additional practice of nirmama toward those involved (not treating the adversary's victory as a personal loss) and vigata-jvaraḥ (releasing the fear of disgrace) come into play. Two levels, same root.

self_assessment: All six renderings anchored in supplied bhāṣya prose with explicit glosses cited; Śrīdhara payload was clean Sanskrit (no HTML artifacts); Madhva's metaphysical uniqueness (bhrānti / adhyāropita) and Vallabha's triple-arpaṇa structure faithfully preserved; no fallbacks or invented commentary used.
